text.data-narration: A physical clock is a device that measures time, typically using a mechanical or digital mechanism. In the context of distributed systems, each node or process has its own physical clock, which can be either hardware-based or software-based.

g.text: Hardware-based clocks are typically found in computer systems, where they are used to measure time for various purposes, such as scheduling and timing-related tasks.
Software-based clocks, on the other hand, are implemented using algorithms and data structures that simulate the behavior of a physical clock.

text.data-narration: There are several methods used to synchronize physical clocks in distributed systems. Some of the most common methods include NTP, PDMP, and PDCP.

g.text: NTP is a widely used protocol for synchronizing clocks over the internet.
PDMP and PDCP are also used for synchronizing physical clocks in distributed systems.

g.title: NTP (Network Time Protocol)

text.data-narration: NTP works by having a central server, called a time server, that provides the current time to clients. Clients then update their clocks based on the received time.

g.text: A time server is typically configured to maintain accurate timekeeping and is often synchronized with an atomic clock.
Clients then update their clocks based on the received time, which ensures accurate synchronization.
